concepts.title: Superposition Principle
concepts.description: The electric field at a point due to a set of charges is the vector sum of the electric fields due to each of the charges at that point.

concepts.title: Electric Field
concepts.description: The electric field at a point in an electric field is defined as the force per unit charge at that point.

concepts.title: Electric Potential
concepts.description: Electric potential at a point in an electric field is defined as the work done per unit charge in moving a test charge from infinity to that point.

concepts.title: Electric Field Lines
concepts.description: Electric field lines emerge from positive charges and enter negative charges. The direction of the line at a point is the direction of the electric field at that point. The number of field lines per unit area is proportional to the strength of the electric field.

concepts.title: Electric Field and Electric Potential
concepts.description: Students often confuse electric field and electric potential. Electric field is a result of electric force per unit charge, while electric potential is the potential energy per unit charge. Always remember that electric potential is conservative, but electric field can be non-conservative.

concepts.title: Unit of Electric Field
concepts.description: The unit of electric field in the SI system is Newton per coulomb (N/C) or volt per meter (V/m).

concepts.title: Gauss's Law
concepts.description: Gauss's Law states that the total electric flux through a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed by that surface divided by the permittivity of free space.

concepts.title: Electric Dipole
concepts.description: An electric dipole is a pair of equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance. The electric field at a point due to a dipole is given by the formula E = (2p / 4πε₀r³)cosθ, where p is the dipole moment, ε₀ is the permittivity of free space, r is the distance from the dipole, and θ is the angle between the dipole and the point.
